sources: docs/core/B2_KERNEL_HARDENING.md X13 — 'Blocked on decision: recovery-auth'; Needed spec work (transfer-resets-arming rule)
docs/core/DECISIONS.md #50 recovery-auth — Status: PROVISIONAL — pending DK ratification
docs/DESIGN.md §10.13 item 1 — 'Still to spec: ... the transfer-resets-arming rule'

inputs.attackFlagText: Double-edged: a thief holding the stolen owner key can transfer-away to void the true owner's in-flight recovery. Whether transfers are restricted during a challenge window is exactly recovery-auth territory — extracting the legacy behavior must not pre-decide that ruling.

fixture.caseTransferVoidsRecoveryDuringWindow: an owner-key-authorized Transfer applied during an open recovery challenge window cancels the pending recovery (withoutPendingRecovery); the now-voided recovery later completes as a NO-OP -> ACCEPT the transfer (under the transfer-supersedes branch of #50)
fixture.caseStaleRecoveryNoOp: the voided recovery, when its challenge window later closes, applies as a no-op (the name already moved by the superseding transfer)
fixture.axisUnderTest: the transfer-vs-recovery precedence axis of #50 (does an owner-keyed transfer during the window supersede the in-flight recovery, or is it restricted/blocked); this is orthogonal to #50-b1, which resolves the recovery signer/evidence shape, not transfer precedence
fixture.priorState: a name with an in-flight recovery; the (possibly stolen) owner key authorizes a transfer-away during the window

inputs.scopeNote: PROVISIONAL on recovery-auth (#50): X13 is candidate-stays and explicitly 'Blocked on decision: recovery-auth' (#50 PROVISIONAL pending DK). The legacy behavior (withoutPendingRecovery on every applied transfer) is the only complete statement, and the flag warns that extracting it must NOT pre-decide whether transfers are restricted during a challenge window — that is the #50 transfer-vs-recovery precedence axis (distinct from #50-b1's signer/evidence-shape resolution). The verdict is derivable conditional on that #50 axis; under the gate it is provisional-vector, never vector-now. Authored under the transfer-supersedes branch (owner-keyed transfer supersedes in-flight recovery -> accept-transfer / stale-recovery-no-op); flips per flipMarker. The transfer-resets-arming rule is also named still-to-spec in DESIGN §10.13 item 1.
inputs.authoringStatus: authored: provisional vector with decisionDeps + flipMarker; status locked (per-area adversarial review passed; authorityTier stays provisional — flips/re-derives on DK ratification of #49/#50); flips on DK's #50 transfer-vs-recovery precedence ruling.

expected.invariant: under the transfer-supersedes branch of #50, an owner-key-authorized Transfer applied during an open recovery challenge window supersedes the in-flight recovery: it cancels the pending recovery (withoutPendingRecovery) and the voided recovery later completes as a no-op; the transfer is accepted; identical on deterministic replay (extracting the legacy withoutPendingRecovery behavior must not pre-decide whether transfers are restricted during the window — that is the #50 transfer-vs-recovery precedence axis; the transfer-resets-arming rule is still-to-spec per DESIGN §10.13 item 1)
expected.rationale: X13 is candidate-stays and explicitly 'Blocked on decision: recovery-auth' (#50, verified PROVISIONAL — pending DK ratification). The legacy behavior (withoutPendingRecovery on every applied transfer) is the only complete statement, and the flag warns extracting it must not pre-decide whether transfers are restricted during a challenge window — that is recovery-auth (#50) transfer-vs-recovery precedence territory. So a verdict is derivable conditional on #50, but it depends on the provisional decision; under the gate it is provisional-vector, never vector-now. The transfer-resets-arming spec PR is also named as still-to-spec in DESIGN §10.13 item 1.

flipMarker.flipsTo: rederive: if DK rules the transfer-restricted-during-window branch of #50 (a Transfer applied during an open recovery window does NOT cancel or override the pending recovery — one possible disposition under the b2h custody design, though the operative axis is the transfer restriction itself, not b2h per se), the verdict flips to reject the transfer-away (or block the void) and the thief-transfer-voids-recovery case no longer stands; the owner-key-authorization shape is stable, only the during-window transfer-vs-recovery precedence moves with the #50 ruling.
